Question: Should I leave my stable senior analyst role to join an early-stage AI startup as Head of Data?
Context: I am 34, have 9 years in financial analytics at a Fortune 500 firm, and have received an offer from a Series A AI startup (18 months runway) offering 20% more base salary plus 0.4% equity. I have a mortgage and one child. My current role is secure but offers no equity and limited upside. The startup is building LLM tooling for compliance teams — a space I find genuinely exciting. My partner is supportive but risk-averse.
Constraints: Cannot take a salary cut. Need health insurance from day one. Cannot relocate — fully remote required.
Stakes: High — affects financial stability, career trajectory, and family security for the next 5+ years.

Question: Should we launch our MVP in Q3 or wait until Q4 after the competitor releases their v2?
Context: We are a 6-person B2B SaaS team with a workflow automation product at 80% feature-complete. Our main competitor has announced a v2 launch in September. Our beta users (12 companies) are highly satisfied, NPS 72. Engineering says Q3 is achievable but would require skipping two planned integrations (Slack and HubSpot). Q4 launch allows those integrations but means 3 more months of burn at /month. We have 14 months runway.
Constraints: Budget cannot exceed current burn. Engineering team cannot scale before Q1 next year. Must not launch without at least 5 paying customers signed.
Stakes: High — launch timing directly affects market positioning, runway consumption, and first-mover perception.

Question: Should we sign a 3-year lease for a dedicated office or stay fully remote for another year?
Context: Our 22-person company has been fully remote for 3 years. Team survey shows 60% want optional office 2-3 days/week, 25% strongly prefer full remote, 15% want full in-office. We have been offered a 3-year lease at /month in a prime location. Competitor firms in our sector are returning to hybrid. We recently lost 2 candidates who cited lack of office culture. Attrition last year was 18%.
Constraints: Cannot exceed /month all-in for office costs. Must not mandate attendance — must remain optional. Lease must have a break clause at 18 months.
Stakes: Medium — affects culture, talent acquisition, and operating costs but company is not at risk.

Question: Should we acquire a smaller competitor for .1M or invest that budget in building the same capability in-house over 12 months?
Context: We run a marketing analytics platform and have identified a niche competitor with 400 active users and a clean codebase (reviewed by our CTO). Asking price is .1M. Building in-house is estimated at 14 months and .8M all-in. The competitor's tech solves our top-3 requested feature. Their team is 4 people — 2 want to stay post-acquisition, 2 are uncertain. We have .5M cash on hand.
Constraints: Must not drop below  cash reserves. Acquisition cannot distract core engineering team for more than 6 weeks. Must close within 90 days if we proceed.
Stakes: High — shapes product roadmap and market position for 3+ years.

Question: Should we migrate from Salesforce to HubSpot to cut costs, or negotiate a better Salesforce contract?
Context: We pay /year for Salesforce across 45 seats. HubSpot equivalent is /year. Migration estimate is 6 months and  in consulting fees. Our sales team (30 people) are Salesforce power users — 8 have Salesforce certifications. We have 220 custom fields, 15 automation flows, and 4 third-party integrations. Two sales reps have already complained about change fatigue after a recent ops restructure.
Constraints: Cannot disrupt active sales pipeline during Q4. Must maintain all existing integrations. Budget for migration consulting capped at .
Stakes: Medium — significant cost saving potential but high execution risk and team disruption.

Question: Should we switch from per-seat pricing to usage-based pricing to better align with customer value?
Context: We are a B2B API platform with 310 customers. Current average contract value is ,200/year per-seat. Top 20% of customers use 10x more API calls than bottom 20% but pay the same. Three enterprise prospects said per-seat pricing was a blocker. Finance projects usage-based could increase ARR by 15-30% over 18 months but introduces revenue unpredictability. Our sales team is nervous about explaining a more complex model.
Constraints: Must not reduce revenue in year 1. Existing customers must be grandfathered for 12 months. Cannot require a full engineering rebuild.
Stakes: High — affects ARR predictability, sales motion, customer retention, and competitive positioning.
